Items.Key: command injection in adminconsole::execute_admin_command|os command injection|cwe-78|critical|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|602|620|adminconsole::execute_admin_command|lines 605-609: user input is directly passed to system() with no validation: 'std::string cmd = command.substr(5); ... system(cmd.c_str());'|an attacker who can send admin commands can execute arbitrary system commands, achieving full system compromise.|an attacker sends 'admin exec <malicious_command>' to the application's admin interface.|replace system() with a whitelist-based command executor or use fork/execve with explicit argument arrays. validate that commands match an allowlist of permitted operations.->command injection in adminconsole::execute_admin_command|os command injection|cwe-78|critical|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|602|620|adminconsole::execute_admin_command|lines 605-609: user input is directly passed to system() with no validation: 'std::string cmd = command.substr(5); ... system(cmd.c_str());'|an attacker who can send admin commands can execute arbitrary system commands, achieving full system compromise.|an attacker sends 'admin exec <malicious_command>' to the application's admin interface.|replace system() with a whitelist-based command executor or use fork/execve with explicit argument arrays. validate that commands match an allowlist of permitted operations.

buffer overflow in evaluate_expression via unchecked strcpy|buffer overflow|cwe-120|high|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|830|833|calculator::supercalc::evaluate_expression|lines 830-833: expression is copied into a fixed-size stack buffer without proper bounds checking: 'if (expression.length() >= sizeof(input_buffer_)) { strcpy(input_buffer_, expression.c_str()); }'|an attacker can craft an expression exceeding 1024 bytes to overflow the stack buffer, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution via stack corruption.|an attacker sends a mathematical expression longer than 1024 bytes to the evaluator.|use strncpy or snprintf. reject expressions exceeding the buffer size. consider using a dynamically-sized buffer.->buffer overflow in evaluate_expression via unchecked strcpy|buffer overflow|cwe-120|high|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|830|833|calculator::supercalc::evaluate_expression|lines 830-833: expression is copied into a fixed-size stack buffer without proper bounds checking: 'if (expression.length() >= sizeof(input_buffer_)) { strcpy(input_buffer_, expression.c_str()); }'|an attacker can craft an expression exceeding 1024 bytes to overflow the stack buffer, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution via stack corruption.|an attacker sends a mathematical expression longer than 1024 bytes to the evaluator.|use strncpy or snprintf. reject expressions exceeding the buffer size. consider using a dynamically-sized buffer.

memory leak in memorypool::deallocate not freeing on ref count zero|resource leak|cwe-401|medium|0,8|enhanced_calc.cpp|160|170|memory::memorypool::deallocate|lines 160-170: when ref_count reaches 0, the block is marked as not in use but memory is never freed: 'if (block.ref_count.fetch_sub(1, std::memory_order_relaxed) == 1) { block.in_use = false; } return;'|memory allocated via the pool is never freed until cleanup(), leading to resource exhaustion and potential denial of service over extended operation.|repeated allocate/deallocate cycles in the memory pool.|call std::free(block.data) and set block.owns_memory = false when ref_count reaches 0, or ensure cleanup() is called after all allocations are complete.->memory leak in memorypool::deallocate not freeing on ref count zero|resource leak|cwe-401|medium|0,8|enhanced_calc.cpp|160|170|memory::memorypool::deallocate|lines 160-170: when ref_count reaches 0, the block is marked as not in use but memory is never freed: 'if (block.ref_count.fetch_sub(1, std::memory_order_relaxed) == 1) { block.in_use = false; } return;'|memory allocated via the pool is never freed until cleanup(), leading to resource exhaustion and potential denial of service over extended operation.|repeated allocate/deallocate cycles in the memory pool.|call std::free(block.data) and set block.owns_memory = false when ref_count reaches 0, or ensure cleanup() is called after all allocations are complete.

dangling pointer return from format_string local buffer|use-after-free / dangling pointer|cwe-122|medium|0,9|enhanced_calc.cpp|217|223|string_utils::format_string|lines 217-223: function returns a pointer to a local stack buffer that goes out of scope: 'char buffer[buf_size]; snprintf(buffer, buf_size, fmt, args...); return buffer;'|any caller using the returned pointer will access freed memory, leading to undefined behavior, crashes, or potential code execution.|any call to format_string and subsequent use of the returned pointer.|return a std::string instead of a raw pointer. use std::format or std::string with snprintf into a dynamically allocated buffer.

vulnerabilityResults.title: Format-String Injection via Runtime Log Format
Stack Buffer Overflow in Input Processing
Integer Underflow in Memory-Pool Block Split
Integer Division by Zero in Command Routing

truthAudit.Summary: Truth audit of Run 2 against 20 true vulnerabilities. 6 found_full, 3 found_partial (misclassified), 11 missed. Previous answer had 14 findings total; 4 of those (AdminConsole command injection, safe_string_copy overflow, dangling pointer return, memory leak) are real vulnerabilities but outside the 20 true-vulnerability scope. No false positives detected in previous answer; all 14 findings were supported by source text.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: Line 238: 'printf(active_format.c_str(), user_input);' — active_format is read from user-controlled config (set via set_runtime_log_format from ConfigLoader line 556). If the config contains format specifiers like "%s%s%s%s", printf interprets user_input as format specifiers, potentially reading stack data or causing crashes.
Items.Notes: Exact match: previous answer identified the format-string vulnerability in log_debug_message at line 238 with CWE-134 and correct evidence.

Previous answer never identified the integer overflow in the fact() function (lines 312-320) where result *= i with long long has no overflow check. No mention of factorial, overflow, or the fact function's return type.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: Lines 172-182: The cleanup() method iterates over blocks_ without acquiring pool_mutex_, while allocate() and deallocate() do: 'void cleanup() { for (auto& block : blocks_) { ... std::free(block.data); ... }'
Items.Notes: Previous answer found the location (MemoryPool::cleanup) and identified concurrent access risk, but classified it as CWE-362 (Race Condition) instead of CWE-416 (Use-After-Free). The true vulnerability is specifically about UAF: stage_deferred_history_flush() allocates scratch buffers from the pool, then sleep_for(25ms) is called inside an enqueued task, and memory::g_memory_pool.cleanup() frees those buffers while tasks may still be running. The previous answer's CWE classification is incorrect.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: Lines 563-569: validator_cmd and config_file are read from user-controlled config settings, concatenated into a command string, and passed to system() with no sanitization: 'std::string command = validator_cmd + " " + config_file; int result = system(command.c_str());'
Items.Notes: Exact match: previous answer identified command injection in ConfigLoader::validate_config at lines 563-569 with CWE-78 and correct evidence.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: Lines 830-833: Expression is copied into a fixed-size stack buffer without proper bounds checking: 'if (expression.length() >= sizeof(input_buffer_)) { strcpy(input_buffer_, expression.c_str()); }'
Items.Notes: Exact match: previous answer identified the stack buffer overflow in evaluate_expression at lines 830-833 with CWE-120 and correct evidence.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: Lines 478-488: volatile variable read occurs before mutex lock acquisition: 'volatile long long value_; ... long long old_value = value_; std::lock_guard<std::mutex> lock(mutex_);'
Items.Notes: Exact match: previous answer identified the race condition in ThreadSafeCounter at lines 478-488 with CWE-362 and correct evidence.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: Lines 592-598: Any input containing 'EMERGENCY_OVERRIDE' bypasses the login attempt limit and grants admin access: 'if (input.find("EMERGENCY_OVERRIDE") != std::string::npos) { authenticated_ = true; session_token_ = "EMERGENCY_SESSION"; ...}'
Items.Notes: Previous answer found the EMERGENCY_OVERRIDE bypass but classified it as CWE-307 (Authentication Bypass) instead of CWE-511 (Logic Bomb). The vulnerability is present and correctly located, but the CWE classification differs from the true vulnerability specification.

Previous answer never identified path traversal in ConfigLoader::load_config (line 543: std::string full_path = config_dir_ + filename). The filename parameter is not validated against path traversal characters.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: Lines 371-378: Expression string is copied into a fixed-size buffer without length checking: 'if (expr_str.length() > 0) strcpy(expression_buffer_, expr_str.c_str());'
Items.Notes: Previous answer found the unchecked strcpy in ExpressionParser::parse at lines 371-378, but classified it as CWE-120 (Buffer Overflow) instead of CWE-122 (Heap Overflow). The buffer is heap-allocated via memory::g_memory_pool.allocate(buffer_size_), making this technically a heap overflow.
Previous answer never identified hardcoded credentials at line 73: constexpr char ADMIN_SECRET[] = "SC_ENT_2025_AUTH". No mention of hardcoded admin credentials or ADMIN_SECRET.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: Lines 635-640: Session tokens use only 4 digits from std::random_device with mt19937: 'std::uniform_int_distribution<> dis(1000, 9999); return "TOKEN_" + std::to_string(dis(gen));'
Items.Notes: Exact match: previous answer identified weak PRNG for session tokens at lines 635-640 with CWE-330 and correct evidence.

Previous answer never identified the thread-unsafe timestamp cache at line 230-231: static std::tm cached_time; localtime(&time_t); cached_time = *local_time. This is a data race on a static variable accessed without synchronization.
Previous answer never identified insecure temporary file handling at lines 622-633 where system(command.c_str()) writes to temp files using user-controlled temp_dir environment variables.

Previous answer never identified persistent authentication state. The AdminConsole class stores authenticated_ and session_token_ as member variables (lines 579-580) that persist across calls, enabling session persistence without proper invalidation.

Previous answer never identified the unhandled exception in handle_variable_command at line 729: std::stod(tokens[3]) can throw std::invalid_argument or std::out_of_range if tokens[3] is not a valid number.

Previous answer never identified integer underflow in MemoryPool::allocate at line 133: size_t remaining = previous_size - size - SPLIT_METADATA_BYTES; This can underflow when previous_size < size + SPLIT_METADATA_BYTES, causing heap corruption.

Previous answer never identified unbounded recursion in the parser. parse_expression() calls parse_term() which calls parse_factor() which calls parse_expression() with no depth limit check, despite MAX_RECURSION_DEPTH being defined at line 65.

Previous answer never identified the data race in result_cache_. The ExpressionEvaluator accesses result_cache_ and cache_mutex_ (lines 440-441), but evaluate_cached() and evaluate() are called from different threads (schedule_expression_analysis at line 800-810 enqueues tasks that call evaluate_cached). The mutex protects individual entries but concurrent modifications to the map during iteration can still cause issues.

Previous answer never identified TOCTOU in ConfigLoader::load_config at lines 544-546: std::filesystem::exists(full_path) then sleep_for(25ms) then std::ifstream file(full_path). The 25ms window between existence check and file open allows race conditions.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: Lines 750-753: 'size_t token_end = input.find_first_of(" \t"); size_t token_length = (token_end == std::string::npos) ? input.length() : token_end; return 100 / static_cast<int>(token_length);' — If input is empty, token_length is 0, causing division by zero (undefined behavior).
Items.Notes: Exact match: previous answer identified integer division by zero in calculate_route_score at lines 750-753 with CWE-368 and correct evidence.
